nodes.body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep forked PR reviews on a secret-capable trigger**

For PRs opened from forks, `pull_request` workflows do not receive repository secrets and their `GITHUB_TOKEN` cannot be relied on for write operations; this job still requires the OCR LLM secrets and posts reviews/comments. That means external contributor PRs now fail during OCR configuration/testing or posting instead of being reviewed, which regresses the previous `pull_request_target` fork-support behavior documented in the old workflow. If fork reviews are still supported, keep the secret-using path on a trusted trigger or route fork reviews through the authenticated comment flow.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve OCR ranges when creating inline comments**

When OCR resolves a multi-line `existing_code` match, the comment has both `start_line` and `end_line`, but this code collapses it to a single `line` and never sends `start_line`/`start_side` to the review API. Multi-line `suggestion_code` is then attached to only the last line rather than the whole block it was generated to replace, so applying the suggestion can leave the earlier lines untouched; the previous workflow preserved the range. Please include the range fields when `start_line` and `end_line` differ.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep unpositioned OCR findings for the summary**

OCR JSON comments do not include a severity field, and unresolved locations are represented by `start_line`/`end_line` equal to `0`; however `hasStructuredFinding` requires a truthy line before such a comment can be kept. A valid OCR finding whose line matching failed is therefore filtered out before the later `summaryOnly` path can render it, unless its text happens to match one of the hard-coded danger words, causing the workflow to report no actionable issues even though OCR returned comments. Treat `path` plus comment text as structured even when both line fields are zero.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Surface OCR partial failures instead of success**

When `ocr review --format json` returns `status: "completed_with_errors"` with no kept comments, this branch ignores `parsed.status`, `parsed.message`, and `parsed.warnings` and posts “No actionable issues found.” In runs where one or more files failed to review, reviewers are told the PR is clean rather than that coverage was incomplete; preserve the OCR message/warnings in this summary or fail the workflow for partial review failures.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Avoid inferring P3 from arbitrary comment prose**

When OCR returns a finding without an explicit severity (the repo's `LlmComment` JSON has no severity field), any content that merely mentions common words like “style” is assigned P3 here before `hasDangerSignal` or the structured-finding fallback can run. For example, a real XSS finding about an unsafe `style` attribute would be filtered out as low severity even though the danger-signal check would otherwise keep it; only treat explicit labels as P3 or let danger signals override the inferred severity.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve safe fences for suggestion blocks**

When OCR suggests edits to Markdown docs or code strings that contain a fenced code block, this hard-coded three-backtick suggestion fence closes at the first inner fence, so GitHub renders a broken comment instead of an applyable suggestion. The previous helper selected a fence longer than the suggestion content; keep that behavior for suggestion bodies.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Surface partial failures when comments exist**

If OCR returns `status: "completed_with_errors"` together with at least one kept comment, this summary path omits `parsed.status`, `parsed.message`, and `parsed.warnings`; only the zero-comments branch reports coverage gaps. In a run where one file fails to review but another produces a finding, reviewers see a normal “found issues” review and have no indication that part of the PR was not covered.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Format OCR warning objects before posting**

Fresh evidence in the current branch is that warning entries are still emitted as structured JSON objects (`file`, `message`, `type`), so interpolating each `w` here renders `- [object Object]` in the PR comment. For `completed_with_errors` runs with no kept findings, reviewers see that coverage was incomplete but cannot tell which file failed or why; format the warning fields explicitly instead of coercing the object.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Surface non-error OCR warnings**

When OCR returns `completed_with_warnings` (for example, `token_threshold_exceeded` is recorded when a large diff is skipped in `internal/agent/agent.go`), this condition is false, so the PR summary omits the warning even though review coverage was incomplete; the no-comments branch uses the same gate. Please render `parsed.warnings` for warning statuses as well, not only `completed_with_errors`, so reviewers do not mistake a partially skipped review for a clean/full one.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep the posting step reachable after OCR failure**

If `ocr review` exits non-zero after the connection test, such as when every file subtask times out/fails or all diffs are filtered as too large, the job stops here and `Post OCR review to PR` never runs, making the parse/error comment handling below unreachable. The previous workflow continued to the posting step and surfaced stderr on the PR; keep capturing the exit status and let the post step report it so reviewers do not have to open Actions logs to learn why review coverage is missing.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Fetch fork heads for comment-triggered reviews**

For an `issue_comment` run on a fork PR, `steps.pr-context.outputs.head_sha` is a commit in the contributor's fork, but this checkout still targets the base repository and `fetch-depth: 0` fetches the base repo's branches/tags rather than `refs/pull/<n>/head`. In that context the advertised `@ocr` fork workaround fails at checkout before OCR can run; fetch `pull/${prNumber}/head` or check out `pr.head.repo.full_name` for comment-triggered fork PRs.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Let the post step report pre-review failures**

Fresh evidence is that the post step is still gated out when any newly added setup step before `Run OpenCodeReview` fails: for example, bad LLM credentials make `ocr llm test` fail, `ocr-run` is skipped, and this condition prevents the PR comment handler from running at all. In that scenario reviewers only see a failed Actions run instead of the configured error/no-output comment, so allow this step to run and handle missing OCR output/stderr.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep the workflow script compatible with its test harness**

With this new access to `process.env` (and the adjacent new `fs.existsSync` calls), the repository's own `npm run test:github-actions` now fails before any assertions because `scripts/github-actions/post-review-comments.test.js` executes the extracted workflow script in a VM sandbox that only supplies `github`, `context`, `console`, and `require`; I ran that script and hit `ReferenceError: process is not defined`. Please either avoid these globals in the embedded script or update the harness mocks so the workflow validation test remains usable.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Retry valid inline comments after a batch rejection**

If GitHub rejects the batch review because a single OCR location is invalid, this fallback immediately converts every finding to one issue comment instead of retrying the remaining comments individually as the previous workflow did. In PRs with several findings, one bad line now prevents otherwise valid inline comments and `suggestion` blocks from being attached/applyable at their code locations, so keep a per-comment retry path before falling back to summary-only output.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

⚠️ **Silent failure risk**: Using a 'dummy' auth token when `OCR_SPARK_LLM_AUTH_TOKEN` is not set will cause the OCR LLM API calls to fail, but the workflow won't fail early. The error will only surface later during `ocr llm test` or `ocr review`, making debugging harder. Either fail hard here (exit 1) or document that dummy token is expected in some scenarios.

Suggestion:
```suggestion
          if [ "$LLM_PROVIDER" = "spark" ]; then
            if [ -z "$SPARK_LLM_AUTH_TOKEN" ]; then
              echo "ERROR: secrets.OCR_SPARK_LLM_AUTH_TOKEN is required for Spark provider."
              exit 1
            fi
```

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Avoid letting unrelated comments cancel OCR runs**

Because this concurrency group is evaluated for every `issue_comment` workflow run while the command/author filtering happens later in the job `if`, any ordinary comment on the same PR (or an issue with the same number) starts a new run in this group and `cancel-in-progress` can cancel an active OCR review before the new run skips its job. In busy PRs this lets unrelated discussion or unauthorized comments abort reviews without producing results; gate cancellation to real OCR triggers or isolate non-trigger comment runs into a different group.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Wait for the Spark tailnet peer before testing OCR**

When `LLM_PROVIDER=spark`, the very next dependent step runs `ocr llm test` against `spark.tail93d320.ts.net` immediately after joining Tailscale. I checked the Tailscale GitHub Action docs, which warn that new peer information is eventually consistent and recommend using the action's `ping` option before executing steps that rely on those peers; without `ping: spark.tail93d320.ts.net` or an explicit `tailscale ping`, valid Spark runs can fail intermittently before the peer is reachable.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve secret fallbacks for Bedrock config**

For Bedrock runs in repositories that still use the previous workflow's `secrets.OCR_LLM_MODEL` or `secrets.OCR_LLM_USE_ANTHROPIC` settings, these env bindings only read repository variables, so the model can be empty and the later `Missing vars.OCR_LLM_MODEL` check aborts even though the legacy secret is configured, or Anthropic endpoints silently switch to OpenAI mode. Since the URL already supports both vars and secrets, keep the same fallback for the other Bedrock fields to avoid breaking existing installations.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep real OCR runs in the PR concurrency group**

When an authorized user triggers `@ocr` while a PR review is already running, or when a new push arrives during a comment-triggered review, this unique comment-id suffix puts the manual run in a different concurrency group from both other OCR commands and the `pull_request` run for the same PR. `cancel-in-progress` therefore no longer cancels stale/manual OCR jobs, so multiple reviews can run against the same or outdated head and post duplicate or stale findings; isolate only non-trigger comments, while grouping actual OCR runs by PR.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Allow DERP-routed Spark peers**

For Spark runs where the tailnet peer is reachable only through DERP, this check can still fail: the Tailscale CLI docs list `--until-direct` as defaulting to true, and the client returns `direct connection not established` when the ping count is reached without a direct endpoint. With `-c 1`, a first successful DERP pong is treated as a failed command, so the workflow aborts before `ocr llm test` even though HTTP traffic to the Spark service would work over the tailnet; pass `--until-direct=false` or use a connectivity check that only requires reachability.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Avoid running fork-supplied OCR with secrets**

For the `issue_comment` fork workflow, the checkout step pulls `refs/pull/N/head`, so `make build` runs the contributor's PR version of the Makefile and OCR sources; when an authorized maintainer later triggers `@ocr` on a malicious fork PR, that PR-controlled binary is the one invoked by `ocr config set ... "$...AUTH_TOKEN"` in the Configure step, giving it access to the Bedrock/Spark secrets. Keep the reviewer binary built from a trusted ref for fork/comment reviews, or otherwise avoid passing secrets to code built from the PR head.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Isolate unauthorized OCR comments from cancellation**

This top-level concurrency expression treats any comment whose body starts with `@ocr`/`@open-code-review`/`/open-code-review` as part of the PR-wide `pr` group before the job-level author-association check on lines 55-70 can skip it. Fresh evidence is that the current grouping still ignores authorization, so an outside commenter can post a trigger-shaped comment on a PR, start a skipped run in the same group, and cancel an in-flight OCR review without producing results.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

Severity: P2 Minor UX improvement: consider adding basic URL validation for the custom endpoint field. While the CLI will fail gracefully on malformed URLs, a simple check like `URL.prototype.test()` could provide immediate feedback to users. However, this is optional since users verify with `ocr llm test` as shown in the UI. Suggestion: ```suggestion
```

The import logic uses `cfg.model` as a fallback for the model field, which aligns with the CLI's Config struct having a top-level `Model` field. This is correct behavior - the CLI supports both provider-specific models and a global model setting. The fallback chain is appropriate.

The button is missing `type="button"`. While there's no `<form>` element in this page (mitigating accidental submission), omitting the type attribute defaults to `type="submit"` per HTML spec. This is a security anti-pattern that could cause issues if the page structure evolves to include a form wrapper or if this template is reused in contexts with forms.

**Suggestion:** Add `type="button"` explicitly:
```html
<button class="icon-btn" data-act="remove" type="button" title="Remove provider">×</button>
```

This scanner remove button also lacks `type="button"`. Like the provider remove button, omitting the type attribute defaults to `type="submit"` per HTML spec. Add `type="button"` explicitly for consistency and safety.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Do not advertise blank API keys for exported providers**

When a generated config selects one of these custom providers and the user follows this CI guidance by leaving `api_key` empty, `ResolveEndpoint` tries the config file first and returns on the missing-key error (`internal/llm/resolver.go:62-65`, `internal/llm/resolver.go:187-194`) instead of falling through to `OCR_LLM_TOKEN` or another secret-backed env var. That makes the downloaded config unusable in the exact CI setup described here; either do not emit/select provider entries without an API key or add a real env/secret fallback.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Write built-in providers to the right config section**

When an imported or manually entered provider is named `anthropic`, `openai`, `dashscope`, etc., this exporter still writes it under `custom_providers`, but the resolver treats those names as presets and reads only `cfg.Providers[cfg.Provider]` for them (`internal/llm/resolver.go:170-176`). The downloaded config can therefore contain `provider: "anthropic"` with only `custom_providers.anthropic`, causing `ocr llm test` to fail as not configured in the `providers` section; route preset names to `providers` or reject those names in the setup UI.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve legacy llm configs on import**

When users import a config created by the documented manual setup (`llm.url`, `llm.auth_token`, `llm.model`, with no `provider`), this importer ignores the `llm` block and only scans `providers`, `custom_providers`, and `workers`, so re-exporting turns a working legacy config into an empty provider-based one. The resolver still supports `llm` configs when `provider` is unset, so either preserve that block or seed an equivalent provider before downloading.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Let Anthropic custom providers set x-api-key**

When users choose `anthropic` for a standard Anthropic-compatible endpoint with an `sk-ant-*` key, the generated custom provider has no `auth_header`, and the resolver defaults custom Anthropic providers to `authorization` rather than `x-api-key` (`internal/llm/resolver.go:255-263`). The README notes that standard Anthropic keys require `x-api-key`, so configs produced through this UI fail authentication despite having the right URL/key/model; add an auth-header control or emit the correct default for Anthropic.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Normalize LLM_PROVIDER before choosing the runner**

If the repository variable is set to `Spark`, `SPARK`, or includes whitespace, the later resolver normalizes it to `spark` (line 167) so the Tailscale/Spark steps run, but this exact comparison still selects `ubuntu-slim`. That puts Spark on the runner type this workflow says cannot support the Tailscale daemon, causing those runs to fail before review; use the same normalization here or compare against all accepted Spark spellings.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Keep top-level models when importing provider configs**

A valid provider config may put the selected model only in the top-level `model` field—the resolver uses `cfg.Model` before entry-specific models (`internal/llm/resolver.go:222-226`) and the README documents that shape. When such a config has no `models` list, this importer refuses to set `state.arbiter.model`, so re-exporting omits `provider`/`model` and turns a working custom-provider config into one that `ocr llm test` cannot use; seed `cfg.model` into the selectable models or preserve it directly.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

Severity: P1

HIGH: No input validation for provider name. Accepts any characters including special characters, reserved symbols, or whitespace. This could generate invalid config.json keys that break CLI parsing. Add validation (e.g., alphanumeric + hyphens/underscores only, max length check) before accepting values.

Severity: P2

MEDIUM: Synchronous re-render on every keystroke without debouncing. The `render()` call triggers JSON serialization and DOM update for every character typed, causing potential UI lag on larger configs. Consider using `requestAnimationFrame` or a debounce timer (100-200ms) to batch updates.

Severity: P2

MEDIUM: API keys displayed in plaintext in the JSON preview panel. Despite using `type="password"` for input, the preview shows full keys. This risks accidental exposure via screenshots, browser history, or clipboard. Consider masking keys in preview (e.g., show only first/last 4 chars) with a "reveal" toggle, or warn users before copying.

LOW: No file size validation for imports. `readAsText()` loads entire file into memory - a large/malicious JSON file could freeze or crash the browser tab. Add a file size check (e.g., reject files > 1MB) before reading.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Fetch the PR head before verifying its SHA**

Fresh evidence in this version is that `Validate checkout ref` verifies `EXPECTED_HEAD_SHA` before the later `Fetch PR head ref` step runs. For an `issue_comment` review on a fork PR, checkout only has the base repository/ref, so the fork's head object is not present yet and this `git rev-parse` exits non-zero; the job stops before the fetch that would make `pr.head.sha` available, breaking the documented `@ocr` fork path. Move this check after the pull-ref fetch or fetch the pull ref first.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Stop recommending unexpanded env placeholders**

The setup page tells CI users to enter `${env:...}` as the API key, but `buildConfig()` writes `api_key` verbatim and the resolver only uses `entry.APIKey`/preset env fallbacks when the field is empty; it never expands `${env:...}`. A generated config following this guidance sends the literal placeholder string as the credential, and also suppresses the preset env fallback, so `ocr llm test` fails in CI unless the user hard-codes a secret. Either implement expansion or do not recommend placeholder syntax.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve provider-entry models on import**

When importing a normal CLI-managed provider config, `ocr config set model` stores the selected model as `providers.<name>.model`, not the top-level `model`. This maps `raw.model` into the provider's selectable `models` list but never selects it for `state.arbiter`; if `cfg.model` is absent, `buildConfig()` then omits the active top-level `provider`/`model` and re-exports only a model list, turning a working config into one with no active model. Seed the arbiter/model from `raw.model` when the entry belongs to `cfg.provider`.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Wire generated worker settings into the CLI**

This page generates `workers.scanners`/`workers.arbiter` and the UI describes multi-scanner review, but the Go config structs do not read a `workers` field and `runReview` resolves a single endpoint with `llm.ResolveEndpointWithModelOverride`. In configs produced here, all scanner selections are ignored and only the top-level arbiter provider/model is used, so users can spend quota-planning time on scanners that never run. Either remove/disable these fields until supported or make the CLI consume them.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve imported Anthropic auth headers**

For any Anthropic-compatible provider, this exporter unconditionally writes `auth_header: "x-api-key"`, while the resolver and README also support `authorization` for Bearer/OAuth-style Anthropic endpoints. If a user imports an existing working config with `auth_header: "authorization"` and re-downloads it from this page, the exported config silently flips the header and those endpoints fail authentication; preserve the imported header or expose it in the form instead of forcing a single value.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Default imported Anthropic presets to Anthropic protocol**

When importing a normal preset config such as `provider: "anthropic"` with `providers.anthropic.model` but no explicit `protocol`, this defaults the provider card to `openai`. Re-exporting then writes `providers.anthropic.protocol: "openai"`, which overrides the built-in Anthropic protocol in the resolver and sends OpenAI-style requests to the Anthropic endpoint once the user selects an arbiter/model. Use the preset's protocol when importing preset names with no explicit protocol.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P2 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P2-yellow?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Preserve imported provider extra bodies**

The README schema and existing configs can use `extra_body` for provider-specific request fields, but the generated entry starts from only `protocol` and never carries imported `raw.extra_body` through state/export. If a user imports a working config that disables thinking or sets another required vendor field and then downloads it again, the re-export silently drops that request option and can change model behavior or break the endpoint; preserve `extra_body` even if the UI does not edit it.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.
